content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 867 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • 1109 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
10 SOUTH BROADWAY • SUITE 957 • LOS ANGELES, 90012

October 27, 1980

Mr. Alan Shubin
Silicon Road
2555 Roeding Boulevard
Salinas, California 93901

Dear Mr. Shubin:

This letter is to confirm our telephone conversation last
week on October 21 and October 23, 1980.

On October 21st, you asked how a major donor reported to
the FPPC must made directly to a candidate for State
Di Division for State Senate campaign, which is in the
process of being "loaned" to a candidates committee, and
what the treatment of such loan is reported since the
major donor will be equal in 1980?

I advised that one major donor reports the amount
to the vendor as an expenditure affected a brief memorandum to
the candidate serving [illegible] and only add [illegible]
that statement that the payment is to be reported
as a loan to the [illegible] campaign or entity.

When the Dnid [illegible] reports the major d [illegible]
one cannot [illegible] will be reported as Section [illegible]
form 901 as a [illegible] loan.

On Wednesday, October 23, you asked whether or not
a major donor must report a "loan" by a candidate. The
position is that the major in question is to be reported as
a loan by a candidate with reference to the amount over
paid to the major donor.

I advised you that the amount involved the major
donor is not reportable because a major donor does not
owe money for political purposes. Therefore, a
major donor does not report [illegible] loaned.

[illegible] shall not [illegible]

A-SO-150

Alan Stobin
Page 2 & 3                                    October 27, 1989


Finally, you asked if a major donor should be listed on a form to a committee
when the committee repairs with the IRS and interest accrues to the major donor? I want to
know if you are any insurance can be purchased to protect the
major donor in Section 'B' of this Bill.

I hope this information has been helpful. If you
please do not hesitate to contact me regarding any
additional assistance.

                                            Sincerely,


                                            [signature]
                                            Alan P. Arnold
                                            Political Reform
                                            Operational


[illegible]

sections.question_synthetic: The letter addresses three questions regarding how major donors report payments made on behalf of campaigns, refunds received from campaigns, and interest earned on loans to campaign committees.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Major donors must report direct vendor payments as expenditures (with loan notation if applicable) and repayments/interest as reportable items on Form 461 Section III, but refunds received are not reportable since major donors do not raise money for political purposes.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is a confirmation letter of prior oral advice rather than a response to a written request. Contains three distinct advisory questions answered sequentially. Date discrepancy noted (letter dated October 17, 1980 but references October 21-22 conversations and October 27, 1986 signature line). No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections present.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: The letter addresses three questions regarding how major donors report payments made on behalf of campaigns, refunds received from campaigns, and interest earned on loans to campaign committees.

CONCLUSION: Major donors must report direct vendor payments as expenditures (with loan notation if applicable) and repayments/interest as reportable items on Form 461 Section III, but refunds received are not reportable since major donors do not raise money for political purposes.

embedding.summary: FPPC confirms prior telephone advice regarding reporting obligations for major donors under various scenarios: vendor payments treated as campaign loans, refunds from overpaid fundraiser events, and interest earned on campaign loans.
